notes: First broad pass across plants, mushrooms, tree products, roots, flowers, and fruit.
Targets contradictions, duplicate concepts, category issues, unsafe wording, and missing critical caution text.
This is not the final complete audit, but it is a real cross-category cleanup layer rather than mushroom-only patching.

records.edibility_detail: Inedible because it is hard and perennial, not because it is a classic poisoning mushroom.
Edible young only. Use only tender margins; mature brackets become tough and fibrous.
Choice only when the classic firm white-orange aborted form is fresh, solid, and correctly identified.
Edible with caution after thorough cooking, but some people still react badly. Old, waterlogged, or undercooked mushrooms raise the risk.
Edible, but quality is strongly age-dependent. Best when young, firm, and not bug-ridden.
Choice edible when fresh and white. Overripe specimens yellow, soften, and lose quality.
Choice edible when still white and tender.
Classified as non-culinary because texture and bitterness make it a preparation mushroom, not a sauté mushroom.
Not a practical edible. People usually value it for tea, extracts, dye, or identification interest rather than food texture.
Listed as inedible in the culinary sense because texture quickly turns leathery and cork-like, though it has a history of traditional utility uses.
Rated inedible as food. It is a sterile conk-like mass, usually used for tea-like preparations rather than as a table mushroom.
Rated inedible because of texture rather than classic poisoning. It is a perennial conk that becomes dense, fibrous, and hoof-like.
Only the firm white aborted form is commonly considered edible by experienced foragers. Soft, brown, or obviously decaying material should be avoided.

records.notes: Actually a lichen rather than a true mushroom. Keep mainly as a survival / emergency food reference, not a normal edible target.
manual_review_reasons: category should be reviewed: lichen, not mushroom

records.notes: Actually a lichen rather than a true mushroom. Keep mainly as a survival / emergency food reference, not a normal edible target.
manual_review_reasons: category should be reviewed: lichen, not mushroom

records.notes: Duplicate concept with wild leek / ramp. Keep one field-facing ramp entry later and merge duplicate records during deeper audit.

records.notes: Generic fiddlehead record overlaps ostrich fern fiddleheads and risks encouraging unsafe generalization. Prefer ostrich fern entry for beginner-facing edible guidance.
manual_review_reasons: generic fiddlehead entry overlaps safer ostrich fern-specific entry

records.edibility_detail: Cook thoroughly before eating. Do not eat raw fiddleheads. Use only true ostrich fern, not generic fiddleheads.

records.notes: Strong lookalike caution needed. Wild carrot can be confused with poison hemlock and other dangerous Apiaceae.

records.edibility_detail: Use only with very strong identification confidence. Apiaceae mistakes can be serious.
Traditional food use exists for very young peeled shoots, but this plant needs careful identification and skin protection because sap can irritate sun-exposed skin.
Not edible raw. Use only with correct species identification and proper boiling / cooking; avoid confusing it with dogbane.
Not edible raw. Historical use involved careful preparation; this is not a casual beginner plant.
Mild salad green, but like spinach and sorrel it contains oxalates and is best not treated as an unlimited bulk food.
Tangy edible herb in small amounts, but the oxalic acid content means it is better used as a garnish than a bulk green.
Tangy edible herb in small amounts, but the oxalic acid content means it is better used as a garnish than a bulk green.
Only true daylilies (Hemerocallis) belong here. Some people get digestive upset even from true daylilies, especially in quantity.
Ripe cooked berries are the usual food form. Raw berries and especially stems, leaves, and seeds can cause digestive upset.
Flowers are widely used for fritters, teas, and cordials, but avoid heavy use of green stems or leaves.
Eat only ripe fruits inside mature husks. Unripe fruits and green plant parts should be avoided.

manual_review_reasons: missing from current audit target if present elsewhere

records.medicinal_uses: Traditionally used in small amounts; the plant is aromatic and salicylate-related, so it should not be treated as a casual bulk food or medicine.
Traditional flavoring and historical medicinal plant; modern use is usually conservative rather than high-volume medicinal use.
Traditional digestive herb, but not a plant for heavy medicinal use.
Traditional wound and skin-support plant; also used in teas for mild soothing support.

records.notes: This entry needs regional fit review. True Gaylussacia huckleberries are not the same thing as all northern Vaccinium berries.
manual_review_reasons: regional fit / exact northern occurrence review recommended
